title: Companies Act, 2013 — Section 135: Corporate Social Responsibility
summary: Mandates companies meeting specified thresholds to spend at least 2% of their average net profits on CSR activities through a board-constituted CSR Committee.
details: Section 135 of the Companies Act, 2013 requires companies with net worth of Rs 500 crore or more, or turnover of Rs 1000 crore or more, or net profit of Rs 5 crore or more during any financial year to constitute a CSR Committee of the Board. The Committee must recommend CSR activities, expenditure amounts, and monitor the CSR policy. The Board must ensure that the company spends at least 2% of the average net profits of the preceding three years on CSR activities. If the company fails to spend the required amount, the Board must disclose the reasons in its annual report. Unspent amounts must be transferred to a specified fund. The CSR framework has transformed corporate engagement in social development and is a critical funding source for NGOs and social work organizations in India.
key_points: Applies to companies meeting specific financial thresholds
Mandatory 2% spending on CSR activities
CSR Committee of the Board to oversee implementation
Unspent amounts transferred to specified government funds

title: Companies Act, 2013 — Schedule VII: CSR Activities
summary: Lists the eligible activities that qualify as CSR expenditure under the Companies Act, covering poverty alleviation, education, health, environment, and social welfare.
details: Schedule VII of the Companies Act, 2013 lists activities that qualify as CSR expenditure. These include: (i) eradicating hunger, poverty, and malnutrition; (ii) promoting education and vocational skills; (iii) promoting gender equality and empowering women; (iv) ensuring environmental sustainability; (v) protection of national heritage and art; (vi) measures for the benefit of armed forces veterans and war widows; (vii) training for promoting rural sports and Paralympic sports; (viii) contribution to the Prime Minister's National Relief Fund; (ix) contributions to technology incubators; (x) rural development projects; (xi) slum area development; (xii) disaster management; and (xiii) contributions to research and development in social welfare. The schedule has been expanded over time to include more areas including COVID-19 response, disaster management, and contributions to specified research funds.
key_points: List of eligible CSR activities under the Act
Covers health, education, environment, and social welfare
Includes rural development, gender equality, and disaster management
Expanded over time to include emerging areas

title: Companies (CSR Policy) Rules, 2014
summary: Provides the procedural framework for CSR implementation including policy formulation, monitoring, reporting, and impact assessment under Section 135.
details: The Companies (CSR Policy) Rules, 2014 provide detailed procedures for implementing Section 135 of the Companies Act. The rules require companies to formulate a CSR policy, constitute a CSR Committee, and disclose CSR activities in the Board's Report. They specify that CSR activities must be undertaken in project/programme mode and cannot include activities undertaken in normal course of business. The rules allow companies to implement CSR through registered trusts, societies, or Section 8 companies, and permit collaboration with other companies for joint CSR programmes. The 2021 amendment introduced mandatory impact assessment for CSR projects exceeding Rs 5 crore, registration of implementing agencies, and provisions for CSR in international jurisdictions. These rules are essential reading for NGOs and social workers seeking CSR partnerships.
key_points: Procedural framework for CSR implementation
CSR through registered implementing agencies
Mandatory impact assessment for large projects
Registration required for CSR implementing agencies

title: United Nations Sustainable Development Goals (SDGs), 2015-2030
summary: A set of 17 interconnected global goals adopted by all UN member states to end poverty, protect the planet, and ensure peace and prosperity for all by 2030.
details: The Sustainable Development Goals (SDGs) are 17 goals and 169 targets adopted by the United Nations General Assembly in September 2015 as part of the 2030 Agenda for Sustainable Development, succeeding the Millennium Development Goals (MDGs). The goals include No Poverty (SDG 1), Zero Hunger (SDG 2), Good Health and Well-being (SDG 3), Quality Education (SDG 4), Gender Equality (SDG 5), Clean Water and Sanitation (SDG 6), Affordable and Clean Energy (SDG 7), Decent Work and Economic Growth (SDG 8), Industry, Innovation and Infrastructure (SDG 9), Reduced Inequalities (SDG 10), Sustainable Cities and Communities (SDG 11), Responsible Consumption and Production (SDG 12), Climate Action (SDG 13), Life Below Water (SDG 14), Life on Land (SDG 15), Peace, Justice and Strong Institutions (SDG 16), and Partnerships for the Goals (SDG 17). India has aligned its national development programmes including MGNREGA, Swachh Bharat Mission, and National Health Mission with SDG targets. NITI Aayog monitors SDG progress through a detailed index. The SDGs provide a universally accepted framework for social work interventions, monitoring, and advocacy.
key_points: 17 interconnected goals addressing global challenges
Adopted by all UN member states for 2015-2030
India aligns national schemes with SDG targets
Provides common framework for development monitoring

title: United Nations Convention on the Rights of the Child (UNCRC), 1989
summary: A comprehensive international human rights treaty establishing civil, political, economic, social, health, and cultural rights of children, ratified by India in 1992.
details: The UNCRC is a human rights treaty adopted by the UN General Assembly in 1989 that sets out the civil, political, economic, social, health, and cultural rights of children. It defines a child as any person under 18 years of age. The Convention is based on four core principles: non-discrimination (Article 2), best interests of the child (Article 3), right to life, survival, and development (Article 6), and respect for the views of the child (Article 12). India ratified the UNCRC in 1992. The Convention has influenced Indian legislation including the Juvenile Justice (Care and Protection of Children) Act, 2015, the Right to Education Act, 2009, and the Protection of Children from Sexual Offences (POCSO) Act, 2012. UNCRC is the foundational framework for all child rights work in India.
key_points: Comprehensive child rights treaty covering all aspects
Four core principles: non-discrimination, best interests, survival, and participation
India ratified in 1992
Influenced JJ Act, RTE, and POCSO legislation

title: Convention on the Elimination of All Forms of Discrimination Against Women (CEDAW), 1979
summary: An international treaty adopted by the UN General Assembly that defines discrimination against women and establishes an agenda for eliminating such discrimination, ratified by India in 1993.
details: CEDAW is often described as the international bill of rights for women. Adopted in 1979 by the UN General Assembly, it defines discrimination against women as any distinction, exclusion, or restriction on the basis of sex that impairs women's human rights and fundamental freedoms. The Convention covers women's rights in political participation, education, employment, health, marriage, and family relations. India ratified CEDAW in 1993 with reservations on Articles 5(a) and 16(1) regarding family and marriage. CEDAW was used by the Supreme Court in the landmark Vishaka judgment (1997) to frame guidelines on sexual harassment at the workplace, establishing that international conventions can be used to fill legislative gaps. CEDAW provides the framework for women's rights advocacy, gender budgeting, and anti-discrimination legislation in India.
key_points: International bill of rights for women
Defines and prohibits discrimination against women
India ratified in 1993 with reservations
Used by Supreme Court in Vishaka case for POSH guidelines

title: United Nations Convention on the Rights of Persons with Disabilities (UNCRPD), 2006
summary: An international treaty promoting, protecting, and ensuring the full and equal enjoyment of all human rights by persons with disabilities, ratified by India in 2007.
details: The UNCRPD was adopted by the UN General Assembly in 2006 and entered into force in 2008. It represents a paradigm shift from the medical model of disability (treating persons with disabilities as objects of charity) to the social model (recognizing them as subjects with rights). The Convention is based on principles of dignity, autonomy, non-discrimination, full participation, and accessibility. India was an early signatory and ratified the Convention in 2007. The UNCRPD directly influenced India's Rights of Persons with Disabilities (RPwD) Act, 2016, which expanded the list of disabilities from 7 to 21, increased reservation from 3% to 4%, and adopted the social model of disability. The Convention covers accessibility, legal capacity, independent living, education, health, employment, and participation in political and public life.
key_points: Paradigm shift from medical to social model of disability
Based on dignity, autonomy, and full participation
India ratified in 2007
Influenced RPwD Act 2016 expanding disability categories

title: Universal Declaration of Human Rights (UDHR), 1948
summary: A milestone document adopted by the UN General Assembly proclaiming the inalienable rights and fundamental freedoms inherent to all human beings, regardless of race, colour, religion, sex, or other status.
details: The UDHR was adopted by the UN General Assembly on 10 December 1948, now observed as Human Rights Day. It consists of 30 articles covering civil, political, economic, social, and cultural rights. Key articles include: Article 1 (all human beings born free and equal in dignity), Article 3 (right to life, liberty, and security), Article 5 (freedom from torture), Article 7 (equality before the law), Article 18 (freedom of thought and religion), Article 22 (right to social security), Article 23 (right to work and fair wages), Article 25 (right to adequate standard of living), and Article 26 (right to education). The UDHR, along with the International Covenant on Civil and Political Rights (ICCPR) and the International Covenant on Economic, Social and Cultural Rights (ICESCR), forms the International Bill of Human Rights. The UDHR has influenced the Indian Constitution's fundamental rights and directive principles.
key_points: Foundational human rights document of the UN
30 articles covering civil, political, economic, social, and cultural rights
Forms part of the International Bill of Human Rights with ICCPR and ICESCR
Influenced the Indian Constitution's rights framework

title: International Covenant on Economic, Social and Cultural Rights (ICESCR), 1966
summary: A multilateral treaty adopted by the UN General Assembly recognizing economic, social, and cultural rights including the right to work, health, education, and adequate standard of living.
details: The ICESCR was adopted by the UN General Assembly in 1966 and entered into force in 1976. It recognizes rights including: right to work and just conditions of work (Articles 6-7), right to form trade unions and strike (Article 8), right to social security (Article 9), right to protection of the family (Article 10), right to adequate standard of living including food, clothing, and housing (Article 11), right to highest attainable standard of physical and mental health (Article 12), right to education (Articles 13-14), and right to participate in cultural life (Article 15). India acceded to ICESCR in 1979. The Covenant's provisions on the right to food, health, housing, and education have been cited by Indian courts in interpreting the right to life under Article 21. The ICESCR provides the legal basis for advocacy on socio-economic rights and welfare entitlements.
key_points: Treaty covering economic, social, and cultural rights
Rights include work, health, education, housing, and food
India acceded in 1979
Cited in Indian courts for interpreting Article 21 rights

title: International Covenant on Civil and Political Rights (ICCPR), 1966
summary: A multilateral treaty recognizing civil and political rights including the right to life, freedom of speech, fair trial, and participation in public affairs, ratified by India in 1979.
details: The ICCPR was adopted by the UN General Assembly in 1966 and entered into force in 1976. It recognizes rights including: right to life (Article 6), freedom from torture (Article 7), freedom from slavery and forced labour (Article 8), right to liberty and security (Article 9), rights of detained persons (Article 10), freedom of movement (Article 12), rights of non-citizens (Article 13), right to fair trial (Article 14), freedom of thought, conscience, and religion (Article 18), freedom of expression (Article 19), right to peaceful assembly (Article 21), freedom of association (Article 22), right to participate in public affairs (Article 25), equality before the law (Article 26), and minority rights (Article 27). India acceded to ICCPR in 1979 with reservations on Article 9 (compensation for unlawful arrest). The First Optional Protocol provides for individual complaints to the Human Rights Committee. The ICCPR rights are largely reflected in Part III (Fundamental Rights) of the Indian Constitution.
key_points: Treaty covering civil and political rights
Rights include life, liberty, fair trial, speech, and assembly
India acceded in 1979 with reservations
Mirrored in Indian Constitution's fundamental rights

title: Paris Agreement on Climate Change, 2015
summary: A landmark international treaty under the UNFCCC adopted at COP21 to limit global warming to well below 2°C and pursue efforts to limit it to 1.5°C above pre-industrial levels.
details: The Paris Agreement was adopted by 196 parties at the UN Climate Change Conference (COP21) in Paris in December 2015 and entered into force in November 2016. The agreement requires all parties to submit Nationally Determined Contributions (NDCs) outlining their climate action plans and to progressively increase ambition every five years. India ratified the Paris Agreement in 2016 and submitted NDCs including reducing emissions intensity by 33-35% by 2030, achieving 40% cumulative electric power capacity from non-fossil sources, and creating an additional carbon sink of 2.5 to 3 billion tonnes of CO2 equivalent through forest and tree cover. The Agreement established mechanisms for transparency, adaptation, loss and damage, climate finance (US $100 billion per year by developed countries), technology transfer, and capacity building. Climate justice is an emerging area of social work, as climate change disproportionately affects poor and marginalized communities in India.
key_points: Global treaty to limit warming to well below 2°C
Nationally Determined Contributions (NDCs) framework
India committed to 33-35% emissions intensity reduction by 2030
Climate finance and technology transfer mechanisms

title: Beijing Declaration and Platform for Action, 1995
summary: A landmark blueprint adopted at the Fourth World Conference on Women for achieving gender equality and empowering women across 12 critical areas of concern.
details: The Beijing Declaration and Platform for Action was adopted unanimously at the Fourth World Conference on Women in Beijing in September 1995, with 189 countries participating. It identifies 12 critical areas of concern: poverty, education and training, health, violence against women, armed conflict, economy, power and decision-making, institutional mechanisms, human rights, media, environment, and the girl child. The Platform for Action calls for governments to adopt gender mainstreaming as a strategy for ensuring that gender perspectives are central to all policy and programme development. India was a signatory and has reported periodically on implementation. The Beijing Platform has influenced Indian policies including the National Policy for Empowerment of Women (2001), the Protection of Women from Domestic Violence Act (2005), various gender budgeting initiatives, and the Beti Bachao Beti Padhao campaign.
key_points: Global blueprint for gender equality adopted by 189 countries
12 critical areas of concern for women's empowerment
Introduced gender mainstreaming as policy strategy
Influenced Indian women's rights legislation and schemes

title: ILO Convention C169 — Indigenous and Tribal Peoples Convention, 1989
summary: An international convention setting standards for the rights of indigenous and tribal peoples concerning land, resources, consultation, and self-governance.
details: ILO Convention C169 is the primary international treaty on the rights of indigenous and tribal peoples, adopted by the International Labour Organization in 1989, replacing the earlier ILO Convention 107 (assimilationist approach). The Convention is based on the principles of consultation, participation, and self-determination. Key provisions include: recognition of indigenous peoples' rights to own and control their traditional lands (Articles 14-15), right to be consulted on projects affecting them (Article 6), right to decide their own development priorities (Article 7), right to retain their customs and institutions (Articles 8-9), and provisions on health, education, and social services (Articles 20-31). India has not ratified C169, but its principles have been cited in Indian jurisprudence including the Samatha (1997) and Orissa Mining Corporation (2013) judgments regarding tribal land rights and the requirement of free, prior, and informed consent. The Convention is a key advocacy tool for indigenous rights organizations.
key_points: International treaty on indigenous and tribal peoples' rights
Based on consultation, participation, and self-determination
Rights to traditional lands and resources
Not ratified by India but cited in land rights jurisprudence

title: Sendai Framework for Disaster Risk Reduction, 2015-2030
summary: A 15-year global framework adopted by UN member states to prevent new and reduce existing disaster risks through economic, structural, legal, social, health, and environmental measures.
details: The Sendai Framework was adopted at the Third UN World Conference on Disaster Risk Reduction in Sendai, Japan, in March 2015, succeeding the Hyogo Framework for Action (2005-2015). It has seven global targets including substantial reduction in disaster mortality, number of affected people, economic losses, and damage to critical infrastructure, and substantial increase in access to early warning systems and disaster risk reduction strategies by 2030. The Framework has four priorities for action: (i) understanding disaster risk; (ii) strengthening disaster risk governance; (iii) investing in disaster risk reduction for resilience; and (iv) enhancing disaster preparedness for effective response and build back better. India has aligned its National Disaster Management Plan (2016, updated 2019) with the Sendai Framework. The Framework emphasizes a people-centred approach and the inclusion of vulnerable groups including persons with disabilities, women, children, and the elderly in disaster management planning.
key_points: Global framework for disaster risk reduction 2015-2030
Seven global targets for reducing disaster impacts
Four priorities: understanding risk, governance, investment, preparedness
People-centred approach including vulnerable groups

title: UN Framework Convention on Climate Change (UNFCCC), 1992
summary: An international environmental treaty adopted at the Rio Earth Summit to stabilise greenhouse gas concentrations in the atmosphere and prevent dangerous human interference with the climate system.
details: The UNFCCC was opened for signature at the Rio Earth Summit (UNCED) in 1992 and entered into force in 1994, with near-universal membership (197 parties). The Convention established the principle of common but differentiated responsibilities (CBDR), recognising that developed countries bear historical responsibility for climate change and must take the lead in combating it. The Conference of the Parties (COP) meets annually to assess progress and negotiate agreements. The Convention led to the Kyoto Protocol (1997) which set binding emission reduction targets for developed countries, and the Paris Agreement (2015) which established a universal framework for climate action. India signed the UNFCCC in 1992 and ratified it in 1993. The Convention's principles of CBDR and equity are central to India's climate negotiating position. National Action Plan on Climate Change (NAPCC, 2008) and State Action Plans on Climate Change (SAPCCs) operationalise India's commitments. The UNFCCC framework has significant implications for social work in climate-affected communities, disaster risk reduction, and environmental justice.
key_points: Near-universal membership with 197 parties
Principle of common but differentiated responsibilities (CBDR)
Led to Kyoto Protocol and Paris Agreement
India signed (1992), ratified (1993), NAPCC operationalises commitments

title: UN Convention on the Prevention and Punishment of the Crime of Genocide, 1948
summary: An international treaty defining genocide and obligating signatory states to prevent and punish the crime of genocide, whether committed in peacetime or wartime.
details: The Genocide Convention was adopted by the UN General Assembly on 9 December 1948 (one day before the UDHR) and entered into force in 1951. It defines genocide as any of five acts committed with intent to destroy, in whole or in part, a national, ethnical, racial, or religious group: (a) killing members of the group; (b) causing serious bodily or mental harm; (c) deliberately inflicting conditions calculated to bring about physical destruction; (d) imposing measures to prevent births; and (e) forcibly transferring children. The Convention establishes that genocide is a crime under international law, whether committed in peacetime or war, and obligates parties to enact domestic legislation and try offenders. India ratified the Convention in 1959. The Convention's provisions have been incorporated into Indian law through the IPC and special legislation. The Genocide Convention has been invoked in cases involving communal violence and atrocities against marginalised groups. It is foundational for understanding international criminal law and the obligations of states to protect vulnerable communities.
key_points: Defines genocide as five specific acts with intent to destroy a group
Covers national, ethnical, racial, and religious groups
Genocide is a crime under international law in peace and war
India ratified in 1959

title: UN Convention Relating to the Status of Refugees, 1951 and 1967 Protocol
summary: A landmark international treaty defining who is a refugee, their rights, and the legal obligations of states to protect them, including the principle of non-refoulement.
details: The 1951 Refugee Convention was adopted following World War II to protect individuals fleeing persecution. The 1967 Protocol removed the original temporal and geographic limitations, making the Convention universally applicable. The Convention defines a refugee as someone with a well-founded fear of persecution based on race, religion, nationality, membership in a particular social group, or political opinion, who is outside their country of origin. The core principle is non-refoulement (Article 33), which prohibits returning a refugee to a territory where they face persecution. Other rights include non-discrimination, freedom of religion, access to courts, employment, welfare, and identity documents. India is not a signatory to the Refugee Convention or Protocol, but Indian courts have applied the principle of non-refoulement as part of Article 21 (right to life). India has hosted significant refugee populations including Tibetans (since 1959), Sri Lankan Tamils, Chakmas, and Rohingyas. The Convention is essential for social workers dealing with refugee protection, rehabilitation, and advocacy for asylum-seekers in India.
key_points: Definition of refugee based on well-founded fear of persecution
Principle of non-refoulement prohibits return to danger
India is not a signatory but courts apply non-refoulement under Article 21
India hosts Tibetan, Sri Lankan, Chakma, and Rohingya refugee populations
